JS From Zero EP.17: How Cookies Actually Work in the Browser
Day seventeen of the reincarnation, and this one's all about cookies.

A cookie is, well, a cookie (the word literally means "biscuit" in Chinese, but let's not get distracted). You'll most often see cookies paired with sessions, and the most common place they show up is account login. Cookies actually come in two flavors:

The difference is easy to spot. A memory cookie disappears the moment the user closes their browser. A disk cookie is more like storage (think an external drive or a USB stick) — it holds onto your data until the user manually clears it, or until its expiry time hits and it gets deleted automatically. One more thing: a cookie is capped at around 4KB, so if you're trying to stash something too big or complex, it just won't fit.
In practice, sessions tend to be used for storing the user's UUID info. Usually you'll combine cookies and sessions together to pull off the auth flow, which also stops people from forging cookies.
If you want a deeper dive into cookies and sessions, I'd recommend this article:
白話 Session 與 Cookie：從經營雜貨店開始
But this post leans more toward cookies, since sessions live on the server side anyway.
